title: Content Management System
description: A content management system (CMS) enables the creation and management of content, primarily on websites.
body: As I mentioned earlier, I want to update esquizo.com to bring it in line with current needs. esquizo.com began about 5 years ago as an evolution of Dr. Julià’s professional website; initially, it was a static site, but since 2003, esquizo.com has been running on a modified version of PHPNuke, which is a content management system (http://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/CMS) (abbreviated as CMS). [](http://web.archive.org/web/*/http://www.esquizo.com)[View the history of the different versions of esquizo.](http://web.archive.org/web/*/http://www.esquizo.com) esquizo.com is now in need of a facelift, both externally (graphical interface) and internally (engine), and I am unsure which system to use internally. The important thing is that the new system can meet the site’s needs—both current ones (articles, forums, news, FAQs, inquiries, polls) and new ones (blogs, RSS, etc.)—while also improving the existing features. I’m debating whether to upgrade to a more modern version of [PHPNuke](http://www.phpnuke.org) or use another platform. There are many CMSs on the market, most of which are [open source](http://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Programa_libre). Some CMS systems include Apache Lenya, PHPNuke, WCMS, Mambo, TikiWiki, Typo3, Drupal, and WordPress, and according to [rankings](http://www.opensourcecms.com/index.php?option=content&task=view&id=388&Itemid=143), PHPNuke isn’t among the best. Migrating to a current version of PHPNuke would probably be easier, but given the alternatives, I’m thinking about switching systems. For now, I’m leaning toward **[Drupal](http://www.drupal.org)**, but before deciding, I want to look into it a bit more. Comments on this are appreciated. Well, in the end, no—Drupal wasn’t the one I chose. I’ve decided on [Joomla](http://www.joomla.org), which used to be Mambo (in fact, based on rumors that Mambo would become a paid service, part of the Mambo team formed Joomla to continue under the open-source philosophy).
